file_name: GSITECHNOLOGYINC_11_16_2009-EX-10.2-INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY AGREEMENT between SONY ELECTRONICS INC. and GSI TECHNOLOGY, INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: 1.4 Licensed Patent(s) The term "Licensed Patent" or "Licensed Patents" means any patent or patents issued at any time in any country (including any inventor's certificates, extension, reissued, renewal and reexamined patents), which (1) is based on any invention made on or prior to the Effective Date, including any patents for inventions disclosed in patent applications filed on or prior to the Effective Date, and issuing from any of those patent applications or any patent applications which are continuations, continuing applications, continuations-in-part or divisions of those patent applications, or on any foreign counterpart of any of those patent applications, (2) (a) is owned by SONY or any of its Affiliates or (b) under which SONY or its Affiliates have the right to grant licenses without payment by SONY or any of its Affiliates of additional royalties or other consideration to a third party (excluding payments for inventions made by that third party while employed by SONY or any of its Affiliates), and (3) would, in the absence of a license granted herein, be infringed by making, using, selling, offering for sale, importing or supplying any Competing Products (including, without limitation, the SRAM Products). "Licensed Patents" shall exclude the Transferred Patents.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 Licensed Patent(s) The term "Licensed Patent" or "Licensed Patents" means any patent or patents issued at any time in any country. "Licensed Patents" shall exclude the Transferred Patents.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of specific criteria for defining 'Licensed Patents' creates uncertainty in enforcement. The original definition provided a detailed explanation of what qualifies as a 'Licensed Patent,' including specific conditions related to ownership, royalty payments, and infringement. By omitting these details, it becomes unclear which patents are actually covered under the license. This omission creates a contradiction because later sections of the agreement rely on this definition to specify the rights and obligations of each party regarding the Licensed Patents, specifically on section 3.1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Grant by SONY SONY, on behalf of itself and its Affiliates, hereby grants to PURCHASER a worldwide, non-exclusive, fully paid-up, royalty-free license (a) under the Licensed Patents to make, have made, use, offer to sell, sell, otherwise dispose of, and import any Competing Products (including, without limitation, the SRAM Products); and (b) to use, reproduce, modify, prepare derivative works of, perform, display, and otherwise practice and exploit in any manner any and all of the SRAM Intellectual Property in connection with the use and exploitation of the 3 Transferred Assets, and to make, have made, use, offer to sell, sell, otherwise dispose of, and import Competing Products (including, without limitation, SRAM Products).
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the detailed criteria for defining 'Licensed Patents', creating uncertainty about which patents are covered. This directly contradicts section 3.1, which grants a license 'under the Licensed Patents' without a clear definition, making the scope of the license ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. The original text provided specific conditions related to ownership, royalty payments, and infringement, all of which are essential for defining the scope of the license granted in section 3.1. The omission of these details creates a strong contradiction, as the meaning of 'Licensed Patents' becomes unclear, leading to uncertainty in the rights and obligations under section 3.1. Therefore, the change constitutes a strong in-text contradiction under the 'Omissions' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 8.2 Essential Patents The Transferred Patents listed on Exhibit A comprise the Patents, both U.S. and foreign, owned or claimed by SONY or any SONY Affiliate, that are essential to the conduct of the Business as conducted by SONY.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2 Essential Patents The Transferred Patents listed on Exhibit A comprise the Patents, both U.S. and foreign, owned or claimed by SONY or any SONY Affiliate.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "that are essential to the conduct of the Business as conducted by SONY," the warranty loses its scope and definition. The section provides a warranty that these patents were essential. Without this assertion, the warranty becomes significantly weaker. This omission impacts the meaning of the warranty as a whole, because now it can be argued that the patents are non essential. The contradiction arises because the PURCHASER can no longer rely on the original understanding that the listed patents were critical for the Business.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.5 Sufficiency To the Knowledge of SONY, SONY and each of SONY's Affiliates owns, or has valid rights to use, all the SRAM Intellectual Property material to the conduct of the Business, including, without limitation, the design, development, manufacture, marketing, use, distribution, import, supply, provision and sale of SRAM Products.
perturbation.justification: The original text warranted that the patents listed in Exhibit A were 'essential to the conduct of the Business.' By removing this crucial qualifier, the changed text weakens the warranty's scope. This creates a contradiction with Section 8.5, which warrants that SONY possesses all 'material' intellectual property for the Business. The omission in 8.2 now allows for the possibility that the Transferred Patents are not essential, undermining the assurance in 8.5 that SONY owns or has rights to all 'material' IP. This creates a clear conflict because 8.5 assumes the patents transferred are material, while the modified 8.2 no longer guarantees their essential nature, leading to uncertainty and potential disputes.
